coredata.dc:description: The use of nanomaterials, which is developing as a revolution in various industries, can also be applied to improve agriculture's resiliency and sustainability. Nanomaterials' small sizes <100 nm and exceptional physicochemical properties allow them to diffuse into the plant vasculature and pass through biological barriers to perform their functions in the plant. Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), a new type of carbon nanomaterials with outstanding ultraviolet (UV) absorption, coordinated fluorescence, good biocompatibility, substantial aqueous solubility, low cost, and precise manufacturing, have recently been shown to boost plant growth and photosynthesis. In this review, we will summarize the applications of CDs in modern agriculture as an optical amplifier based on the CDs' exposure methods to plants with soil cultivation, hydroponic cultivation, foliar feeding, and light-harvesting film. Finally, the mechanism, challenges, and future research direction of this field are described in brief.

CDs' most accepted functional mechanisms as an optical amplifier in agriculture. The light-dependent reactions of photosynthesis occur in the thylakoid membrane. In electron transport chains, PSs found in the thylakoid membrane utilize solar energy to produce ATP and NADPH. In addition to the several pigments that each PS carry to assist them in absorbing light energy, the reaction center of the PS also contains a unique pair of chlorophyll molecules. When visible light is absorbed by one of the pigments in PSI and II, energy is transmitted inward from pigments until it reaches the reaction center in PSI and II, where it is then transferred to an electron acceptor in the electron transport chain. CDs can help PS as a pigment by absorbing wavelengths of light that normal pigments cannot absorb (1). Then, CDs convert these lights into visible light usable for the PS (2–1). CDs integrated with up-conversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) to promote NIR light conversion (2–2). In addition, CDs can act as electron donor that transfers electrons to PSI and II, thereby increasing the total electron transport chain in photosynthesis (3). In the meantime, because CD increases light absorption, one of its consequences is the imposition of ROS on the plant more than usual. For this reason, CDs can sometimes scavenge these species as one of the other attractive features (4).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The use of nanomaterials, which is developing as a revolution in various industries, can also be applied to improve agriculture's resiliency and sustainability. Nanomaterials' small sizes <100 nm and exceptional physicochemical properties allow them to diffuse into the plant vasculature and pass through biological barriers to perform their functions in the plant. Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), a new type of carbon nanomaterials with outstanding ultraviolet (UV) absorption, coordinated fluorescence, good biocompatibility, substantial aqueous solubility, low cost, and precise manufacturing, have recently been shown to boost plant growth and photosynthesis. In this review, we will summarize the applications of CDs in modern agriculture as an optical amplifier based on the CDs' exposure methods to plants with soil cultivation, hydroponic cultivation, foliar feeding, and light-harvesting film. Finally, the mechanism, challenges, and future research direction of this field are described in brief.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplying food for humankind and crude substances for industries are related to agriculture. With the increasing population, the problem of food deficiency has become more widespread than ever, since increasing the worldwide agricultural production in 2050 would be 70% [1]. Meanwhile, the decrease in soil organic substances, the nutrient deficiencies [2,3], the loss of freshwater resources [4], and the decline in farmland have all contributed to the problem of a food shortage [5]. Using genetic engineering and agronomic approaches leads to considerable growth in plant production [6,7], which is complicated, costly, and time-consuming. On the other hand, using fertilizers and pesticides to assure agricultural productivity severely pollutes the environment [8,9]. Moreover, the uncertain risk of prolonged exposure associated with eating genetically modified foods raises substantial social issues [10,11].

As is known to all, plants need the solar spectrum as a plentiful natural resource and sustainable energy for growing [12,13], as well as water, soil, and oxygen. Typically, sunlight's blue, red, and far-red light are more essential to plants than UV and green light. In other words, plants can use around 10% sunlight energy for photosynthesis [14]. Therefore, conversion of UV light to visible light can increase plant growth efficiency by enhancing the portion of light energy used for plants. Additionally, this light conversion can potentially reduce the harmful effects of extra UV light irradiation on the earth that cause by ozone layer depletion [15]. Some natural and synthetic light-harvesting agents can absorb UV light and emit visible light. Now, this visible light is absorbed by the plant's chloroplast and then transferred to the photosystems I (PSI) and photosystems II (PSII), which finally heighten the electron-transfer processes and water splitting [16].

Designing ultrasmall nano fertilizers is one way to alleviate global food pressure that has been achieved by developing nanotechnology [17–22]. In the past decade, CDs have emerged as a new class of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial with a size below 10 nm and intrinsic fluorescence properties that provide remarkable advantages, including ease of preparation, appropriate biocompatibility, low toxicity, and high photostability [23–28]. After their first synthesis in 2004 [29], a wide variety of physicochemical and electrochemical methods were introduced to the preparation of CDs [30–34]. Simultaneously, widespread usage of CDs has been proved in various fields, such as biomedicine [35,36], photoelectric devices [37,38], sensors [39,40], and catalysis [41,42]. In addition, CDs have attracted attention as a competent carbon nanomaterial model to study farm plant growth and its viability. Researches and developments are in remarkable progress about the effects of CDs on the modern agriculture and harvesting such as food safety [43–45], food storage [46,47], plant cell imaging probe [25,48], plant growth regulator [49,50], stress tolerance [51,52], plant fertilizer [53,54], pesticide application [55–57], or carrier agent for biomolecules [58]. Finally, using CDs as an optical amplifier for enhancing photosynthesis [59] in the plant system is an area of the largest potential.

A few review articles have been recently published concerning the use of CDs or other carbon nanomaterials in agriculture. These articles have reviewed the various applications of carbon dots in relation to plants or agriculture [49,60–63]. However, given the photosynthetic process's optical nature and carbon dots' remarkable optical properties, this article's topic is dedicated to using CDs to augment photosynthesis and related mechanisms. Additionally, this review summarizes recent advances in applying CDs as optical amplifiers in agriculture. In the following sections, first, CDs' main synthesis methods and significant properties are briefly described. Then, the investigations of CDs as an optical amplifier on plants (in vitro or in vivo) and relevant mechanisms are discussed. Finally, the challenges and future research direction for realizing all the capabilities of CDs in agriculture are also highlighted. This review aims to entice researchers, particularly those outside the plant nanobiotechnology sector, to study nano-enabled agriculture, particularly regarding CD optical characteristics.

Since the introduction of CDs in 2004, several bottom-up and top-down procedures for their preparation have been developed [29]. Chemical and electrochemical oxidation, arc discharge and laser ablation are the most common techniques of top-down procedure to obtain quantum-sized CDs from large carbon structures. In the bottom-up methods, carbonization of small molecule precursors via pyrolysis, hydro/solvothermal, and microwave-assisted methods led to CD formation (Fig. 1 ) [64].

In the top-down approach, various carbon sources such as graphite, activated carbon, and graphene are decomposed to the CDs via chemical/electrochemical oxidation, arc discharge and laser ablation techniques. The first CDs were obtained by purifying the single-walled carbon nanotubes via arc-discharging of soot. After that, the arc-discharge method is widely used for synthesizing CDs because the shapes of prepared CDs can be easily manipulated during this process [29]. In addition, another general method for making CDs with low-cost, high efficiency, high purity, uncomplicated size adjustment, and adequate reproducibility is electrochemical oxidation [64,65]. The first report on CD synthesis based on the electrochemical technique was published in 2007 by Zhou's group [31]. Recently, Ming et al. synthesized high-pure CDs through a one-step electrochemical process by using water as an electrolyte solution. This economical and efficient process makes CDs environmentally friendly [66]. To use low-cost raw materials and enhance production scale, Chen et al. have used an available and inexpensive carbon source to prepare CDs using the electrochemical method [67]. The laser ablation was employed by Sun et al. for the first time on graphite in the vicinity of hot water with argon as a carrier gas [23].

Carbon dots can also be synthesized from small molecules by using of pyrolysis process, hydro/solvothermal reactions, and microwave-assisted synthesis [64]. The hydro/solvothermal method is widely used due to the numerous and low-cost precursors, easy synthesis process, low energy consumption, and independence from large instruments. Carbohydrates, organic acids, juices, or waste peels can be used as carbon sources. In recent years, natural carbon sources have been used more for CDs than conventional sources due to their low-cost, environmentally friendly, and greater accessibility [68–70]. Most hydro/solvothermal methods for CDs need a reactor to seal and react organic precursors when temperatures and pressures are high and the reaction time is extended. Microwaves, electromagnetic waves with a frequency of 300 MHz to 300 GHz, can supply enough energy for breaking chemical bonds of raw materials and are determined as a new way to produce CDs with high quality and high efficiency. In general, the hydro/solvothermal microwave method can effectively shorten the reaction time owing to providing uniform heating and causing uniform distribution in CDs particles size [71,72]. It has been shown that short pulses of external heat can cause the chemical oxidation and carbonization of organic matter by converting it to CDs [73]. Hydro/solvothermal and microwave-assisted synthesis approaches are the most usable in agriculture applications of CDs due to their scalability, short reacting time, and easy application. Utilizing very common and inexpensive precursors are another reason, as well.

Because of many materials and synthesis procedures, various physicochemical properties are available for prepared CDs. However, different CDs have common features such as low toxicity, environmentally friendly, high stability, and adjustable fluorescence [74,75]. This section illustrates the possible effects of CDs on the natural environments and their optical properties.

Plant application is a hot topic in CD material research. In particular, using CDs as a photosynthetic enhancer has attracted more attention. During photosynthesis, chloroplasts can only use the visible spectral range of sunlight with specific absorption. In general, strong light absorption in CDs is in the UV region, and the absorption peaks are concentrated in the range of 260–320 nm [76]. In addition, plant photosynthesis can directly use longer wavelengths (blue, red, and far-red light) than UV light, and some CDs have enhanced absorption in long-wavelength. Therefore, this carbon-based nanomaterial can help plants capture solar energy more efficiently.

The wavelength of fluorescent radiation emitted by CDs has a wide range from deep UV to near-infrared (NIR) region [77–79]. For plant application, the study of emissive CDs is highly desirable. Blue and red CDs synthesized by Li et al. and the emission wavelengths (483 and 682 nm) matched with the absorption of chloroplasts (400–700 nm). Therefore, photosynthetic activity can be increased [59]. Most CDs reported, however, show only short-wavelength emissions [80–82]. Therefore, distinguishing the emission of CDs and autofluorescence of chloroplast is difficult. In addition, the optimum red emission in most CDs is accursed in organic solvents. Besides, the synthesis process of some CDs needs high energy consumption (160–200 °C) for several hours [83–86]. As a result, it is imperative to easily synthesize CDs with high efficiency, water-solubility, and long-wavelength emission [87].

With some CDs applications in agriculture, plants acquire CDs directly to increase photosynthesis, which means that these substances enter the food chain. Recent studies examined CDs cytotoxicity in some organisms such as bacteria, aquatic zebrafish, mice, and rats. It has been determined that CDs are highly biocompatible. After consumption, it does not cause serious pathological effects, and their excretion in zebrafish, rats, and mice will occur via normal metabolic pathways [88–90]. In addition, most investigations have demonstrated that CDs in moderate concentrations have a beneficial effect on plants. It means they will have appropriate growth conditions and tolerate abiotic and biological stresses [91–95]. CDs in optimal amounts can enhance plant growth by speeding up photosynthesis, nutrient uptake, and nitrogen stabilization by Azotobacter [95]. To better understand and evaluate the probable environmental hazards of its execution, it is crucial to ascertain if it has biological toxicity or environmental impact through the planning phases. Considering the fact that the number of investigations on the effect of CDs on biological systems and their environments is not enough, it needs precise assessment in the future. Recently, many efforts have been made to find a green source for synthesizing CDs, mainly from biomass such as fruit juices, food waste, etc. [96–102]. Fig. 2 shows the different raw materials used to make these carbon nanostructures. With a focus on sustainability, toxic and hazardous reagents have been replaced by waste and biomass. However, this issue is still challenging among scientific groups due to the low quantum efficiency of green synthesized CDs.

The rapid development of nanotechnology in the last two decades has dramatically expanded the study of the impact of nanomaterials on agriculture [53,103,104]. This exploration of CDs is new and can be classified into in vitro and in vivo methods explained in the following subsections.

Hybrid systems made of nanomaterials and light-harvesting materials are an alternate method of enhancing sunlight absorption. Combining spinach chloroplasts with light-harvesting aggregation-induced emission carbon dots (CD-AIEgens) resulted in a novel hybrid photosynthetic system. Compared to natural chloroplasts, the CD-AIEgens serve as an optical amplifier and improve photosynthetic effectiveness. Active AIE emission, strong UV absorption, and appropriate light stability are all displayed by CD-AIEgens. The rate of electron transport in PSII is accelerated by CD-AIEgens, which also boosts the activity of chloroplasts by absorbing light at a broader range of wavelengths (Fig. 3 ) [105]. In a different work, microwave-assisted synthesis was used to create and combine CDs with photosynthetic pigments (derived from spinach leaves) to produce a hybrid structure. The findings demonstrated that CDs convey a significant quantity of energy to photosynthetic pigments. As a result, the range of 360–420 nm excitation wavelength interval considerably increases the fluorescence capacity of the photosynthetic pigment chlorophyll. The outcomes demonstrated that CDs might be a crucial element of hybrid structures to boost the ability of light-harvesting complexes in the high-energy region of the visible spectrum to absorb light [106].

In this approach, the seeds germinated and then moved to pots filled with soil. Ultimately, the CDs are sprayed on or supplemented into the soil. A study demonstrated that utilizing biomolecule functionalized CDs, it is possible to realize increased plant yields. The more significant increase of carbon assimilation compared with the operating efficiency of PSII showed enhanced electron transport in the thylakoid membranes, resulting in augmented photosynthesis [108]. In another study, CDs showed a dose-dependent stimulatory effect on the growth of N. benthamiana (Fig. 5 ), which is achieved by increasing photosynthesis. It is indicated that CDs can transfer electrons to the chloroplasts and enhance UV light absorption by N. benthamiana [109].

Foliar feeding involves spraying water-based fertilizers on the foliage of the plant. The rationale for this practice is based on scientific studies from the 1950s that show leaves can absorb minerals through the stomata and cuticles. In fact, foliar feeding is 8, 10, and even 20 times more effective than conventional soil fertilization [113,114]. Recently, it has been shown that the spraying of CDs on plants increases yield and growth [115–117].

Fluorescent polymeric coatings (FPCs), produced by covalent copolymerizing nanomaterials in the foliar application, are more affordable than photosynthesis enhancement using fluorescent nanomaterials alone or physically dispersed in polymeric materials. They also perform consistently and reliably in practical uses. One type of FPCs has been developed in which CDs and tannic acid are covalently linked. After FPCs spraying on tomato leaves, chlorophyll content, practical photochemical efficiency, and growth rate were increased. Light-excited CD nanocapsules can absorb UV light, which leads to producing photoelectrons transmitted to PSI and PSII through electron transfer processes. Ultimately, during water splitting, the formation of oxygen and ATP is promoted in the presence of CD nanocapsules [118]. Furthermore, encapsulating the synthesized CD in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) improves the lettuce photosynthesis process by increasing CD residence time on the leaf. The CDs nanocapsules attached to the leaves emit visible light, which then absorbed by the chloroplast and applied to increase the electron transport speed. Spraying CD nanocapsules on the leaves raises the amount of soluble sugar, production yield, and protein content of lettuce (Fig. 7 ) [119].

The vegetable greenhouse provides growing various products regardless of the season and climate and causes the growth of the highest quality organic vegetables. For greenhouse cultivation, a film covering the greenhouse is essential. In addition, relative to UV, green, and yellow light, which are rarely used, blue and red light are generally absorbed by plants [120,121]. As a result, a new system including a high molecular weight polymer (as container matrix) and appropriate red and blue light emitter (as sunlight converting agent) can be a rational alternative to the standard greenhouse film to enhance the efficiency of greenhouse cultivation.

After several years of continuous research, research in this area has made significant progress. Up to now, the most accepted functional mechanisms of CDs as an optical amplifier in agriculture have come down to an increase in light-harvesting, light-conversion, electron-donating, and ROS scavenging (Fig. 9 ) described in the following subsections.

Previous mechanisms have suggested that CD increases light absorption in plants, especially in the UV region. UV light (200–400 nm) from sunlight can cause ROS production in plants and damage nucleic acids, proteins, and the membrane of cells [130]. Therefore, increasing the photosynthetic light coverage of plants and reducing toxic UV radiation is necessary to produce food. To increase photosynthesis via light conversion, CDs with tunable fluorescence emission under UV light [131,142-144] provide suitable candidates for making photosynthetic nanohybrids [112,115,132] or films [125,126]. Li et al. created CDs that effectively transform UV-A light into far-red emission that can be absorbed and used by chloroplasts ( Fig. 10b). Roman lettuce utilizes a broader range of solar radiation, which results in better photosynthetic activity in both in vitro and in vivo tests [87]. To prepare CD@Mg(OH)2, membrane CDs are mixed by Mg(OH)2 nanosheets. Based on the UV–visible and emission spectra, UV light changes to blue and red light by the light-conversion film. The film's emission spectrum and the chlorophyll a and b absorption spectrum are coordinated. Therefore, sunlight can be converted to blue and red light, which is needed for plant growth using light-conversion film [122]. However, some light-conversion CDs have short-wavelength emissions rather than long-wavelength emissions, such as red and far-red. In addition, some of the CD-based light-conversion agents have not yet been used in natural agriculture.

Light is necessary for photosynthesis. The previous two mechanisms increase light absorption in plants, which may increase their ROS production and harm the photosynthetic apparatus [133]. ROS accumulation is the main cause of stress in plants. To avoid plants from damaging by ROS under normal conditions, ROS production and scavenging are controlled by a dynamic balance and do not harm the plant. This balance is disturbed by increasing the level of ROS under stress conditions. Peroxidation of the lipid membrane, denaturation of protein, and DNA damage of cells is caused by ROS (O2-, H2O2, OH) due to the potent oxidation activity, which inhibits plant growth [134,135]. Effective natural enzymes currently remove ROS; However, these enzymes are sensitive and are impacted by environmental factors. CDs can be used as an alternative in this case because they can scavenge ROS by increasing root activity, chlorophyll level, and antioxidant enzyme activity in plants [52,136]. Yang et al. showed that the ROS fluorescence signal after foliar feeding of CDs was lower than the control group ( Fig. 10c), indicating that CDs may reduce excessive ROS accumulation to protect PS [52]. Another study has also been shown to effectively reduce oxidative stress in Italian lettuce by scavenging intracellular ROS [137]. However, there are limited studies about this function of CDs [140,141].

In photosynthesis, the transfer of electrons during the reaction of light into the electron transfer chain is one of the key and important processes. As previously noted, excessive harvested electrons might result in the creation of ROS [138]. However, the plant may not have harvested enough electrons for light reaction due to the cloudy, rainy, or shady conditions. Improved photosynthesis and plant performance may result from a novel strategy for assisting plants in harvesting more electrons in low-light situations. Nanomaterials can be used to boost plants' ability to capture electrons. Functional density theory (DFT) was utilized by Wang et al. to demonstrate that a CD's internal structure is likely to lose its electrons during ionization ( Fig. 10d). Due to the separation of internal electrons and holes, this result demonstrated that CDs were used as an electron donors. As a result, the efficiency of light-conversion was increased, and electrons were made available to accelerate light and break down water in PSII [115]. In low-light situations, plant photosynthesis might be enhanced by adding electron donor CDs to chloroplasts [139]. Future research should discover other electron donors' nanomaterials and investigate how effectively and precisely they can transfer electrons to chloroplasts.

Table 1 shows precursors, synthesis method, emission color, biocompatibility, in vitro or in vivo test of CDs on the plant, type of plant, and functional mechanism of CDs reported in the literature. In this table, we have tried to summarize the published articles during recent years based on the optical amplifier action of CDs in agriculture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Other promising optical applications of CDs

ce:section.ce:para: Light is vital for plants and regulates their growth. There are several artificial light sources to improve plants' growth, such as incandescent lamps [145], high-pressure sodium lamps [146], and metal halide lamps [147]. These sources suffer from low efficiency, high-energy consumption, and environmental pollution defects [122,148]. LEDs are a novel light source in plant factories with no previous problems and the ability to prevent various diseases and accelerate the growth of plants [149]. In addition, the competence of LEDs as the light source is improved when they are combined with high-quality CDs such as thermally activated delayed fluorescence and room-temperature-phosphorescence CDs. To create CDs-based LEDs, various techniques are available, such as using pure or doped CDs as the emission layer and CDs as the interface transport layer [150]. Recently, dual-emitting CDs/CaAlSiN3:Eu2+-silica were effectively used to construct a multi-wavelength emission LED (red and blue light wavelength) for promoting plant development. The as-prepared CDs based-LED shows two significant emission bands in the electroluminescence spectra at 470 and 612 nm ( Fig. 11a ). Compared with the commercial white LED, reported CDs-based LED to improve the photosynthesis of lettuce and garland chrysanthemum plants followed by the increasing of chlorophyll, carotenoids, nutrients content, and consequently the increase of the total biomass in the experimented plants ( Fig. 11b). The low toxicity, low cost, and high stability are the additional advantages of these CDs-based LEDs [148].

Photosynthesis produces more than 90% of plant biomass, which uses solar energy to fixation CO2 into carbohydrates [154]. However, plants cannot utilize all solar energy because their photosynthetic pigments absorb just a visible range of light [155,156]. Therefore, enhancing imputed solar energy to the photosystems can open new opportunities for modern agriculture and food technologies [157].

The common strategies to interfere with and enhance the photosynthetic efficiency in plants are targeted breeding and gene manipulation, which are very expensive, complex, and time-consuming approaches. These methods are also unavailable for all plants because of the requirement for a high-quality genome sequence and other limitations [158]. Light-conversion plastic films and artificial light sources are the other possible approaches for stimulating plant growth and productivity that are expensive and cause environmental pollution [118]. Recent multidisciplinary biochemical research has focused on nano-enabled methods for improving photosynthesis. Systems that increase photosynthesis using nanomaterials, such as CDs, SWCNTs, CdTe quantum dots, and TiO2 nanoparticles, have been developed [118]. The advantages of fluorescent CDs over other nanomaterials are obvious. These nanomaterials can increase the amount of solar energy available to plants by converting other light rays into visible light, but achieving this purpose has its challenges.
Despite significant advancements in the use of CDs as optical amplifiers in agriculture and food production, more efforts are still required to solve some existing issues:

2) NIR light is another prominent solar spectrum that plants cannot use, which accounts for about 52% of sunlight. The UCNPs can absorb sunlight NIR and emit visible light. In this process, two or more low-energy photons from NIR light are absorbed and converted to high-energy photons with shorter wavelengths (stronger energy) via excited state absorption, energy transfer, or multiphoton absorption [63]. CDs can integrate with UCNPs to promote NIR light conversion and finally enhance plant photosynthesis [132]. However, this application is still mainly at the proof-of-concept stage.

3) For the construction of CDs, numerous affordable synthesis techniques with simple starting materials have been developed. However, the most significant drawback to their application is their low QY. As a result, there is a high demand for CDs that provide a greater yield. In addition, as shown in Table 1, the QY of CDs is not mentioned in most studies, although it has been an important issue in this field.

4) Finally, CDs can also absorb light in the visible light region, and this problem may remove part of the usable light from the reach of the plant. This problem can affect photosynthesis and even reduce it. To use CDs as an optical amplifier in modern agriculture, attention should be paid to this issue, and CDs that have the lowest absorption in the visible light region must be used.

The application of CDs in agriculture and harvesting requires particular consideration of their toxicity and biocompatibility. The carbon core of CDs is responsible for their toxicity in high concentrations [159]. But in low concentrations, they show low toxicity and appropriate biocompatibility (up to 10 μg/mL) [159,160]. CDs' toxicity has been studied, and results show that they are well-biocompatible, do not produce noticeable pathological changes in the body, and can exit the body through normal metabolic pathways [161]. However, a comprehensive and conclusive evaluation of CD phytotoxicity has not yet been provided. Accordingly, it is necessary to determine CD's biocompatibility and toxicity by carefully studying its cytotoxic effect on plant growth [54,107,162]. However, some issues should be considered:

1) Most of the articles found that low concentrations of carbon dots are effective in increasing photosynthesis, while high concentrations do not have a positive effect on plant growth. [60]. Along with CD concentration, even slight variations in raw materials and processing methods can produce profoundly different outcomes [163,164]. Therefore, it is necessary to consider the influence of precursor type, manufacturing method, CDs concentration, and plant type to determine the phytotoxicity of CDs. CDs created using green synthesis techniques are likely helpful in this regard as they are more environmentally friendly [102].

2) Animal cell models (in vitro) are used in the most recent investigations on the toxicity of CDs. Studies on in vivo toxicity in plants and animals should also be conducted. Many animal models (such as mice, nematodes, apes, etc.) can be used to examine the toxicity of CDs. Following CD treatment, it is necessary to investigate the toxicity of intact harvested plants.

3) The study about the optical and physicochemical stability of CDs, which, like some studies done in this field [165–167], should be done in the farm environment. For example, do exposure to light increase the toxicity of CDs? Or are CDs degradable in the environment?

4) Another issue that should be seriously addressed in future studies is how long carbon dots remain in plant tissue after being absorbed.? Knowing the half-time and stability of CDs in the plant tissue is important for two reasons; first, how many times should CDs be added during plant growth? Second, what should be the interval between the last use of CDs and the plant harvesting?

Photosynthesis enhancement is an impressive procedure to achieve more nutrients that are vital for nourishing the population all over the world. As a result, agricultural development using nanomaterials helps reduce the environmental effect of agronomical production as well as maintain/increase plant yield. At low concentrations, CD-based nanomaterials are biocompatible, chemically inert, and even non-toxic to human cells [94,168–171]. Therefore, CD-based nanotechnology can be a promising alternative to sustainable agricultural production. Even though CDs have helped this field make major advancements, more work needs to be done to utilize CDs in agriculture as an optical amplifier. Large-scale synthetic limitations and the high-cost of some CDs precursors currently preclude their agricultural applications. Nature provides many fascinating precursors. Therefore, preparing CDs from natural carbon sources for large-scale synthesis is economical, feasible, and necessary. In addition, most of the CDs described for increasing photosynthesis are absorbed directly by plants, which in turn causes these substances to enter the food chain and is an unknown threat to human health. It is best to incorporate the CDs in a light-harvesting film or LEDs growth plant to prevent these safety issues, which increases photosynthesis without being absorbed by the plant.

In CDs, induction of the redox processes by light is related to various energy conversion schemes that have attracted considerable attention. Strong and widespread absorption over a significant portion of the solar spectrum, adequate charge separation under photoexcitation, and consequently, electron and hole generation can promote chemical reactions such as the reduction of CO2 and the generation of H2 during water splitting. In view of the anticipated advancements in high-yield CO2 photoconversion and water splitting, CD-derived nanostructures may be a suitable platform for photocatalytic energy conversion applications. As a result, CDs may play a significant role in the development of natural and artificial photosynthesis in the future.
Not applicable.
